growth_records.snippet: close to the experimentally determined glucose uptake rate for L. lactis IL1403, 18-24 mmol g-1 h-1 (ref 25)

arginine uptake flux reported as amino-acid flux in Fig 4C (mmol/gCDW/h); direction (uptake) explicit but no numeric value in text

growth_records.cite: Chen et al. Proteome constraints reveal targets for improving microbial fitness in nutrient-rich environments. Molecular Systems Biology (2021). DOI:10.15252/msb.202010093. [PMC8022198]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostat cultivation ... 300 ml bioreactors with 270 ml working volume ... 30C water baths. The headspace was continuously flushed with 5% CO2 and 95% N2. pH was controlled at 6.5 ... CDMpc with 25 mM glucose was added at a rate of 2.25 ml
growth_records.notes: Wild-type L. lactis MG1363, glucose-limited anaerobic chemostat. Growth rate CALCULATED from chemostat steady-state identity mu = D = 0.5/h (dilution rate stated explicitly: feed 2.25 ml/min into 270 ml volume = 0.5/h). Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.5 = 1.386 h. Temperature 30C, pH 6.5, anaerobic (5% CO2/95% N2 sparge). Medium = CDMpc; only 25 mM glucose stated in this paper, full recipe not given (referenced), so medium_composition_stated=false. Glucose and arginine are consumed and lactate/formate/acetate/ethanol are secreted (mixed-acid fermentation, which the text states dominates at D=0.5/h since the total-proteome constraint is not yet active), but the paper reports these exchange fluxes only graphically (Fig 1C measured exchange fluxes; Fig 4C amino-acid fluxes in mmol/gCDW/h) with NO numeric values in the text or tables, so rates left null rather than invented.

growth_records.strain: SB261 and SB17 (kefir isolates; Blasche et al.)
growth_records.medium: CDM35-lactose (chemically defined medium; also CDM42-lactose)

up.units: qualitative (13C tracing; no specific rate reported)
up.method: 13C-based proteomics: L. lactis preferentially utilizes the glucose moiety of lactose
Yeast provides essential amino acids to L. lactis; complex bidirectional amino-acid exchange detected

sec.method: L. lactis releases the galactose moiety of lactose, which is consumed by yeast (cross-feeding)
Lactate produced by L. lactis; ~70% fully 13C-labelled on lactose-[13C-glucose], ~40% on lactose-[13C-galactose]

mfa.note: Circa 70% of pyruvate and lactate fully 13C-labelled during growth on lactose with the glucose moiety labelled
Only ~40% fully labelled when the galactose moiety is labelled, indicating preferential glucose-moiety catabolism by L. lactis
Alanine had ~20% higher labelling when the galactose part of lactose was labelled

growth_records.cite: Gabrielli et al. Unravelling metabolic cross-feeding in a yeast-bacteria community using 13C-based proteomics. Molecular Systems Biology (2023). DOI:10.15252/msb.202211501. [PMC10090948]
growth_records.method: not reported numerically (growth followed as OD/CFU time-courses, means of 3 replicates)
growth_records.snippet: the community was grown on lactose ... with either glucose or galactose fraction labelled with 13C ... Circa 70% of pyruvate and lactate were found to be labelled in all carbon atoms during growth on lactose-[13C-glucose], but only 40% in t
growth_records.notes: 13C peptide-based MFA / cross-feeding study (yeast + L. lactis). NO quantitative specific rates (mmol/gDW/h) or numeric growth rate reported for L. lactis; only concentration/labelling time-courses. Captured qualitative exchange directions from 13C tracing: L. lactis consumes the glucose moiety of lactose and releases galactose (consumed by yeast) plus lactate/pyruvate; yeast provides amino acids to L. lactis. mfa_fluxes here are isotope-labelling percentages, not exchange fluxes. Record retained for the documented cross-feeding topology; rates left null (not quantified).

growth_records.medium: CDMG agar, co-culture spot 2.5 cm from lactate producer LM

up.method: HPLC; derived

sec.units: mmol/L/h (volumetric, 24-33 h window; confounded by LM-diffused lactate)

growth_records.cite: Hui Liao et al. Slower-growing species promote interspecific cooperation and coexistence under acid stress through cross-feeding. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-67395-z. [PMC12816087]
growth_records.snippet: lactate content ... reaching 68.8 mM at 24 h and a maximum of 98.2 mM at 33 h ... the low amount of residual glucose (22.8 mM) at 30 h and consumption was significantly reduced over the next 6 h to 8.66 mM
growth_records.notes: Glucose window 30->36 h: 22.8 - 8.66 = 14.14 mM over 6 h -> 14.14/6 = 2.36 mmol/L/h. Lactate 24->33 h: 98.2 - 68.8 = 29.4 mM over 9 h -> 29.4/9 = 3.27 mmol/L/h. Lactate here includes LM lactate diffusing into the co-culture spot (stated: '68.8 mM ... inconsistency with ... maximum lactate ... in the producer LM at 27 h was due to the lag in diffusion time'), so the lactate rate is NOT purely A01/B01 production - confounded, low confidence. Two-species co-culture; per-species split not resolvable to rates from text.

growth_records.snippet: The total carbon consumption rate increased from 31.7 to 177 C-mmol (gDW*h)-1 with an increase of the specific growth rate (mu) from 0.1 to 0.5 h-1. Most of the carbon was converted to lactate at a rate of 113 C-mmol/(gdw*h) while products
growth_records.notes: A-stat continuous culture, so mu=D=0.1 h-1 at quasi-steady state. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.1 = 6.93 h. Total C consumption 31.7 C-mmol/gDW/h reported directly. Glucose uptake DERIVED: paper states amino acid consumption = 16% of total C-influx at ALL growth rates, hence glucose = 84%; glucose_C = 0.84*31.7 = 26.63 C-mmol/gDW/h; glucose has 6 C, so glucose = 26.63/6 = 4.44 mmol/gDW/h. Pyruvate-group AA consumption 1.4 C-mmol/gDW/h reported directly at mu=0.1. Yxs 0.10 C-mol/C-mol and ATP yield 5.0 gDW/mol-ATP reported at mu=0.1. IMPORTANT: the underlying experimental glucose/amino-acid consumption and fermentation-product formation RATES were measured in and taken from Lahtvee et al. (ref [9], BMC Microbiology; GEO GSE26536); this paper reprocessed them into absolute MFA fluxes via a simplified 103-reaction network. Biomass conversion factor 0.32 g/L per OD unit.
